points.text: Enable request tracing (e.g., X‑Request‑ID) and correlate gateway 502/504 logs with backend logs to locate failing calls.
Collect latency histograms for each downstream service call; identify outliers that exceed SLA thresholds.
Profile database CPU usage; examine slow query logs and execution plans for queries that spike during the issue.
Check connection pool saturation in the backend; increase pool size or implement back‑pressure if connections are exhausted.
Verify circuit‑breaker settings; ensure they open on repeated downstream failures to prevent cascading errors.
Add exponential backoff retries for transient downstream calls, limiting max attempts to avoid overload.
Monitor system metrics (CPU, memory, network) on both gateway and backend; correlate spikes with error timestamps.
Run load tests replicating post‑release traffic to reproduce the bottleneck and measure resource utilization.
If DB CPU remains high, consider query indexing, read replicas, or caching to offload frequent reads.
Deploy temporary rate limiting at the gateway to smooth traffic bursts while investigating root cause.
Enable distributed tracing to measure latency of each downstream call and identify slow services.
Collect metrics on request latency, error rates, DB CPU, thread pool usage, and compare pre‑release baselines.
Verify gateway timeout settings exceed the sum of downstream call timeouts; adjust budgets if needed.
Profile the backend to find hot DB queries; add indexes or rewrite queries to reduce CPU load.
Introduce circuit breakers around downstream services to fail fast and prevent cascading 502/504 errors.
Ensure connection pools and concurrency limits for the database are sized appropriately to avoid saturation.
Temporarily roll back the recent release or disable new features to isolate the change causing the issue.
Consider batching or parallelizing independent downstream calls to improve overall response time.
Implement rate limiting or load shedding at the gateway to protect downstream services during spikes.
Enable pg_stat_statements and identify the top‑CPU queries during the error spikes.
Run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) on those queries to spot sequential scans or high row estimates.
Check for missing indexes on filter/join columns and create covering indexes where appropriate.
Inspect pg_locks and pg_stat_activity for lock contention or long‑running transactions.
Monitor wait_event_type (e.g., IO, lock) to see if CPU is spent on disk reads or lock waits.
Review connection pool settings (max connections, pool size) to avoid saturation of DB resources.
Correlate DB latency with downstream service timings using tracing to ensure the bottleneck is not external.
Enable DB monitoring (e.g., pg_stat_activity, wait events) to spot long‑running queries and high CPU usage.
Inspect lock contention: query pg_locks or equivalent to find sessions waiting on rows or tables.
Analyze execution plans of new queries; add missing indexes or rewrite inefficient joins.
Verify isolation levels; a higher level (SERIALIZABLE) can cause extra locking and CPU load.
Add circuit‑breaker/retry logic for downstream calls to prevent cascading load on the database.
Check load balancer health‑check failures and error rates to see if backends are being marked unhealthy.
Monitor LB request queue depth; a growing queue indicates saturation and can cause 502/504.
Measure per‑endpoint latency of each downstream call from the backend to locate slow services.
Enable DB slow‑query logging and correlate CPU spikes with specific query patterns or load spikes.
Inspect backend connection‑pool metrics for exhaustion or long wait times that could time out requests.
Verify that timeout values are consistent across LB, API gateway, backend HTTP client, and DB driver.
Enable distributed tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) to pinpoint the hop where latency exceeds timeouts.
Review recent release changes for added downstream calls, increased parallelism, or config changes.
If DB CPU is the bottleneck, consider scaling vertically, adding read replicas, or query optimization.
Implement circuit‑breaker or retry‑with‑backoff on downstream calls to prevent cascading 502/504 errors.
Enable TCP metrics (netstat -s, ss) on gateway and backend to detect retransmissions and packet loss.
Capture packet traces (tcpdump) to measure RTT, SYN retries, and identify congestion episodes.
Verify keepalive and timeout settings on load balancer and backend to prevent premature 502/504.
Check connection pool sizes and socket reuse; pool exhaustion can cause delays and gateway timeouts.
Correlate database CPU spikes with increased TCP SYN backlog or slow ACKs from the DB server.
Use latency histograms of downstream calls to isolate which service is adding most delay.
If high retransmission rate, tune TCP window scaling or switch congestion control algorithm.
Ensure proper HTTP keepalive headers to reduce connection churn and improve throughput.
Review gateway timeout thresholds; increase them if backend processing legitimately exceeds defaults.
Isolate or roll back recent code changes to differentiate application bugs from network issues.
Capture per‑endpoint latency histograms (p95/p99) at the gateway and each downstream service to locate slow paths.
Apply Little’s Law: concurrency = throughput × average latency; compare required concurrency to DB CPU‑limited capacity.
Budget total request timeout (e.g., 2 s) and allocate time slices to each downstream call, leaving headroom for retries.
Measure current RPS and DB CPU; if CPU >80 %, estimate max sustainable RPS = current RPS × (80 %/CPU) to avoid overload.
Introduce circuit breakers and exponential back‑off for downstream calls to prevent cascading 502/504 errors.
If a recent release introduced changes, roll back or feature‑flag them and run a load test to confirm the bottleneck is removed.
